title: A sociology of truth?
abstract: Click to increase image sizeClick to decrease image size Notes 1. Though there needs to be some care regarding exactly what is being accepted and rejected here, as is made clear by Bloor's most recent defence of relativism (see Bloor 2007 Bloor, D. 2007. Epistemic grace: Antirelativism as theology in disguise. Common Knowledge, 13: 250–80. [Crossref] , [Google Scholar]). 2. See Williams (2002 Williams, B. 2002. Truth and truthfulness: An essay in genealogy, Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. [Google Scholar], 142–143). For criticism and further references, see Putnam (2001 Putnam, H. 2001. Reply to Bernard Williams' 'Philosophy as a humanistic discipline'. Philosophy, 76(298): 605–14. [Crossref] , [Google Scholar]). 3. This is also a blindspot in Young's (2008 Young, M.F.D. 2008. Bringing knowledge back in: From social constructivism to social realism in the sociology of education, London: Routledge. [Google Scholar]) retrospective account of changes in his position. 4. Young has acknowledged his role in the development of the 'new sociology of education', albeit rather modestly, in a recent interview (Young 2007 Young, M.F.D. 2007. Battle in print: What is education for? Michael Young interview by Toby Marshall Battle of ideas, institute of ideas. http://www.battleofideas.org.uk/index.php/site/battles/1074/ (accessed September 17, 2010) [Google Scholar]). 5. The student movement of the late 1960s is mentioned four times, but its implications are never explored. 6. Equally problematic because 'belief' tends to imply falsity or at least a speculative character.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
